I think you want `.extend` instead of `.append`. `readlines()` returns a list, so using `append` will make `self.this_library` a list of lists. Example:
```python
>>> a = [1,2,3]
>>> a.extend([4,5])
>>> a
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
>>> a.append([6,7])
>>> a
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, [6, 7]]
```

I think _normally_ the right thing to do here would be to call `warning.simplefilter("once", PendingDeprecationWarning)` at the top of the file, and then call `warning.warn("get_char will soon ...", PendingDeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)` from inside  `get_char`. This way the warning would only be printed once instead of every time `get_char` is called (e.g. if it were called in a loop) and you wouldn't have to do the stack frame parsing yourself, but then you wouldn't get the yellow text, so this is probably the best option

@scottpurdy: I looked at this once more and it was definitely worth a second look. When I run `make install` on my MacBookPro, there are two attempts to install the capnp files under the following headings:

1. `Installing: /Users/vkruglikov/nta/nupic.core/build/release/include//capnp`
2. `Installing: /Users/vkruglikov/nta/nupic.core/build/release/include/capnp`

Note that the headings only differ in two slashes versus a single slash before the last component. This corresponds precisely to the two duplicated sets of install commands that only vary by the presence/absence of an extra slash:

From CapnProto.cmake:
```
foreach (INCLUDE_DIR ${CAPNP_INCLUDE_DIRS})
  install(DIRECTORY ${INCLUDE_DIR}/kj
          DESTINATION include/)
  install(DIRECTORY ${INCLUDE_DIR}/capnp
          DESTINATION include/)
endforeach ()

install(FILES ${CAPNP_LINK_LIBRARIES}
        DESTINATION lib/)
```

From src/CMakeFiles.txt:

```
install(FILES ${CAPNP_LINK_LIBRARIES}
        DESTINATION lib)

foreach(directory ${CAPNP_INCLUDE_DIRS})
  install(DIRECTORY "${directory}/capnp"
          DESTINATION include)
  install(DIRECTORY "${directory}/kj"
          DESTINATION include)
```

> What are exports? Exported targets are targets that are necessary to compile against the target doing the exporting. For example, javac needs to know about the super classes of a class in order for another class to inherit from it. If the API of a class inside a target expects subclassing, then the targets that contain that class's superclasses would need to be exported.

> Exports are transitive. This means that if a target exports one of its dependencies and that exported dependency has exports, those exports will also be end up on the classpath. This allows the export graph to model requirements created by type hierarchies.

Indeed at the moment the last example you put would fail. This is because `extend` is simply:
```python
def extend(self, ittr):
    for i in ittr:
        self.append(i)
```
It all really depends on exactly what semantics we want things to have. At the GPI-level I think we want things to be copied gratuitously in order to avoid stale references etc. In this case, we would need to make sure that `GangaList._export_extend` correctly deep-copies the objects before appending them.

No, `charset` is fine, it's what the `Content-Type` header uses.

> How about `encoding_{dynamic,static}`?

The purpose of renaming one or both of the settings is to distinguish them and make them easier to understand without having to read the docs. This proposal does not accomplish that.

As you pointed out at the beginning of this thread, `charset_dynamic` isn't limited to dynamic output, so to lift the confusion we want a name without `dynamic` in it.

Well, my problem is that the decorator is not self-contained. With that approach using the decorator forces the wrapped object to change, I don't think the interface is any different to some predefine attributes in that regard.

> all uses of this cache would use the same lock and this may not be appropriate.

That not how it works. The same closure will use the same objects, but different closures will have different objects:
```python
>>> def a():
...   b = 0
...   def c():
...     nonlocal b
...     b += 1
...     return b
...   return c
...
>>> c = a()
>>> c()
1
>>> c()
2
>>> c()
3
>>> c2 = a()
>>> c2()
1
>>> c2()
2
>>> c2()
3
```

the problem is Alarm task.
immediately after the alarm is started, it tries to get the block number it was started in
`self.last_block_number = self.chain.block_number()`
But if context switch due to i/o block occurs at this point, `last_block_number` is still `None`
If you take a look at `raiden_network` fixture, it calls `poll_for_new_block()` immediately after the channels are ready. If the alarm is still waiting for block, the `poll_for_new_block()` method will attempt to perform arithmetic with `last_block_number` still set to `None`
